planting.note: Cold-moist stratify the seed, then sow on the surface in late winter.

directions: Give the seed a period of cold, damp chilling before sowing it on the soil surface for light. This slender native prairie plant handles average to moist soils and full sun. Slim spikes of small pale blue to white flowers open in early to midsummer above a basal rosette.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost; lift or mulch in cold zones.

directions: Surface sow on damp mix in warmth, leaving the seed exposed to light. This Mexican relative of cardinal flower carries reddish foliage and wants rich, moist soil in sun. Vivid scarlet flower spikes appear in late summer; protect the crown over winter where frosts are severe.

planting.note: Start indoors early; plant out after frost into moist, rich soil.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist mix in warmth, or buy started plants for reliable color. Prized for its deep beetroot-purple leaves, this selection needs constant moisture and full sun to look its best. Scarlet flowers glow against the dark foliage from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Start early indoors for first-year bloom, then transplant after frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface and keep warm and bright for even germination. Bred for compact, uniform habit and first-season flowering from an early start indoors. Give steady moisture and full sun for dense spikes of fiery scarlet bloom through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto damp mix and leave uncovered under bright, warm conditions. This member of the compact Fan series flowers the first year when started early. Water generously and grow in full sun to enjoy rich rose-pink spikes over green foliage all summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for first-season color; transplant after frost.

directions: Surface sow the tiny seed on moist mix and provide warmth and light to germinate. A tidy Fan-series selection with an unusual soft salmon flower color. Keep the soil constantly moist in full sun and shear spent spikes to prompt rebloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early; plant out after frost into moist ground.

directions: Sow seed on the surface in a warm, well-lit spot and keep evenly damp. This Fan-series plant pairs bronze-tinted leaves with deep wine-red flowers. Grow in full sun with reliable moisture for the richest foliage and bloom color from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for first-year flowering.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed and keep it warm and bright without covering it. This taller Compliment-series selection blooms the first year from an early start. Provide moist, fertile soil and full sun for large spikes of intense scarlet through the summer months.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant after frost into rich, damp soil.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix and leave exposed to light in a warm spot. Part of the vigorous Compliment series, it produces tall wands of deep crimson-red bloom. Keep constantly moist in full sun and remove faded spikes to extend the display.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for first-season bloom.

directions: Surface sow the tiny seed and keep it warm, moist, and brightly lit. This Compliment selection offers deep blue flowers, a rarer color in the tall lobelias. Grow in moist, fertile ground in full sun to partial shade for upright spikes in late summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early for first-year flowering; plant out after frost.

directions: Sow on the surface of a moist, warm seed mix and keep in good light. This well-branched Starship-series plant flowers the first season when sown early. Give steady moisture and full sun for large, showy spikes of bright scarlet in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Surface sow the fine seed and keep warm and bright until seedlings appear. A well-branched Starship-series selection with deep rose-pink spikes. Grow in constantly moist, rich soil in full sun and it flowers the first year from an early sowing.

planting.note: Start indoors early; plant out after frost into moist soil.

directions: Sow seed uncovered on moist mix in a warm, well-lit place. This Starship-series plant carries deep burgundy-red flowers on strong, branching stems. Keep the soil constantly moist in full sun for a robust summer show.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery plants in spring in moist, rich ground.

directions: Set divisions or container plants out in spring with the crown level at the surface. This selection is grown vegetatively and needs steady moisture and full sun. Spikes of deep ruby-red flowers rise above green foliage from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist soil.

directions: Plant divisions in spring at the original soil depth into rich, damp ground. This compact, dwarf selection stays low and bushy, good near the front of a moist border. Grow in full sun to light shade for stubby spikes of grape-purple flowers in summer.

planting.note: Set divisions or plants out in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant nursery divisions in spring with the crown at soil level and water in well. This selection is grown vegetatively and wants constantly moist ground in full sun. Bright magenta-pink flowers cover the spikes above green foliage through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set divisions out in spring keeping the crown at the surface, and water generously. This vigorous selection carries dark-flushed foliage and needs steady moisture in full sun. Tall spikes of vivid magenta-purple flowers appear from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile ground.

directions: Plant divisions in spring at the original depth and keep the soil reliably moist. This selection offers rich purple flowers over deep green to bronzed foliage. Grow in full sun to light shade and cut back after flowering to keep the clump vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into moist, rich soil in sun.

directions: Set divisions out in spring with the crown at soil level and water in thoroughly. This selection combines purple-flushed leaves with bright magenta-pink flowers. Give it steady moisture and full sun for the best foliage color and tall summer spikes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist, fertile soil.

directions: Plant nursery divisions in spring at the original depth and water well to settle them. This selection produces large spikes of clear rose-pink flowers over green foliage. Keep the ground moist in full sun and deadhead to prolong the summer bloom.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into moist, humus-rich soil.

directions: Set divisions out in spring with the crown level and keep the soil constantly moist. Grown for its very dark chocolate-purple foliage, this selection thrives in wet ground and full sun. Bright red flower spikes rise dramatically over the near-black leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in spring after nicking the hard seed coat to speed sprouting.

directions: Scarify the seed and soak it overnight, then sow about a quarter inch deep in cool, well-drained soil. Give plants deep roots room and avoid disturbing the taproot once established. Tall flower spikes in mixed jewel tones open from the bottom up in early summer; shear spent stalks to encourage a second flush.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall where the deep taproot will not be moved.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then sow a quarter inch deep in cool, evenly moist soil. This vigorous species self-sows readily and prefers slightly acidic ground. Blue to purple spikes rise above palmate leaves in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in fall for natural cold stratification, or stratify seed before spring sowing.

directions: Scarify and cold-stratify the seed, then sow a quarter inch deep in sandy, lean soil. This native prairie lupine is the sole larval host of the Karner blue butterfly and dislikes rich or wet ground. Blue-violet spikes appear in late spring.

planting.note: Start indoors early since this selection can bloom the first year from seed.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells to protect the taproot, then transplant carefully. This compact, quick-blooming strain flowers the first season if sown early. Clear blue spikes suit the front of a sunny border.

planting.note: Fall sow in sandy ground for overwintering rosettes and spring bloom.

directions: Scarify seed and sow shallowly into loose, sandy soil in fall. This is the more rounded-flowered of the two official Texas bluebonnet species and thrives on lean, dry sites. Soft blue spikes open in early spring.

planting.note: Sow in spring in fast-draining soil; the shrubby plant grows quickly.

directions: Scarify seed and sow a quarter inch deep in sandy, sharply drained soil. This fast, short-lived evergreen shrub tolerates coastal wind and salt but resents heavy clay. Fragrant yellow spikes cover the bush in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in cool, moist soil; very cold hardy.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow a quarter inch deep in cool, evenly moist ground. This northern species handles harsh climates and fixes nitrogen in poor soils. Blue-violet spikes appear in early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in cool soil; also grown as a cover crop and grain.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in cool spring soil where the plants will grow. This annual lupine fixes nitrogen and produces edible seeds after debittering. White to pale-blue flowers give way to broad pods in summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; commonly used as a green manure.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in cool soil in early spring. Fragrant yellow whorled spikes make this both an ornamental and a nitrogen-fixing cover crop. Flowers open in early summer above narrow palmate leaves.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; widely grown as a grain and cover crop.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in cool, well-drained soil in early spring. This blue-flowered annual is a leading nitrogen-fixing forage and green-manure lupine. Slender spikes bloom in late spring to early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; a fast annual for bedding and cutting.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep after the last frost in well-drained soil. This Mexican annual blooms quickly from seed in shades of blue, white, and rose. Cut spikes for the vase when the lowest florets open.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; a protein-rich grain lupine of the Andes.

directions: Sow seed about an inch deep in well-drained soil in spring. Grown for its edible high-protein seeds after soaking to remove bitterness. Bicolor blue-and-white spikes appear in summer atop tall, branching stems.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring in California-style Mediterranean climates.

directions: Scarify seed and sow a quarter inch deep in loose soil in fall or spring. This robust California native tolerates clay and quickly forms lush foliage. Deep blue-purple spikes bloom in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Fall sow into wildflower plantings for spring color.

directions: Scarify and sow the seed a quarter inch deep in well-drained soil in fall. This dainty California annual naturalizes in meadows and thrives on winter rain. Blue and white spikes light up hillsides in early spring.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring in mild-winter regions.

directions: Scarify seed and sow shallowly in loose, well-drained soil. This California annual bears densely whorled spikes, often white to pale yellow or pink. Flowers open in spring and attract native bees.

planting.note: Fall sow for stratification, or stratify before spring sowing.

directions: Scarify and cold-stratify the seed, then sow a quarter inch deep in lean, gritty soil. Silvery hairs coat the foliage of this western native, which tolerates drought once established. Blue to purple spikes bloom in early summer.

planting.note: Fall sow or stratify seed for spring planting in dry sites.

directions: Scarify and cold-stratify the seed, then sow a quarter inch deep in dry, well-drained ground. This silvery-leaved mountain native is well suited to xeric gardens and rangeland restoration. Blue-violet spikes appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Fall sow or stratify; native to western mountain meadows.

directions: Scarify and cold-stratify seed before sowing a quarter inch deep in moist, well-drained soil. This meadow lupine favors mountain streamsides and cool summers. Blue-purple spikes bloom in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in fall in fast-draining soil; a woody California native.

directions: Scarify seed and sow a quarter inch deep in sharply drained, gritty soil. This silvery evergreen shrub needs excellent drainage and minimal summer water. Fragrant blue-purple spikes bloom in spring and support native butterflies.

planting.note: Sow in fall in sandy coastal soils; drought tolerant once set.

directions: Scarify seed and sow shallowly in loose, sandy soil. This silvery coastal shrub of California dunes tolerates salt spray and dry summers. Soft blue-lavender spikes bloom from spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow in fall into sandy coastal ground where it spreads low.

directions: Scarify seed and sow a quarter inch deep in sandy soil near the coast. This sprawling, mat-forming lupine stabilizes dunes along the Pacific shore. Blue to purple spikes rise from trailing stems in late spring.

planting.note: Fall sow into wildflower mixes for early spring bloom.

directions: Scarify and sow the tiny seed a quarter inch deep in well-drained soil in fall. This diminutive western annual pairs beautifully with California poppy in meadow plantings. Small blue-and-white spikes bloom in early spring.

planting.note: Sow in fall or early spring in mild-winter areas.

directions: Scarify seed and sow shallowly in loose, well-drained soil. This western annual bears whorled spikes and puffy seed pods that give it its common name. Yellow, pink, or purple flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring; a vegetatively propagated hybrid.

directions: Plant potted divisions in spring in fertile, well-drained soil and keep the crown at ground level. This bicolor hybrid carries dense spikes of pink and orange over compact foliage. Deadhead spent spikes to prompt rebloom.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring; propagate by division to keep the color.

directions: Plant potted stock in spring in rich, well-drained soil and water to establish. This striking hybrid bears bicolor spikes of violet-blue and yellow. Remove faded spikes to encourage a second flush of bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early or set out nursery plants in spring.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells, then transplant carefully to protect the taproot. This Band of Nobles selection shows bold blue-and-white bicolor spikes. Cut back after bloom for a chance at a second show.

planting.note: Start indoors early or plant nursery stock in spring.

directions: Scarify and start seed in deep pots to spare the taproot when transplanting. This heritage selection bears pink-and-white bicolor spikes over palmate foliage. Deadhead to prolong the display into summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early or set out plants in spring.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells before transplanting to protect the root. This Band of Nobles selection carries rich carmine-red spikes. Shear spent flower stalks to keep the plant tidy and encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early or plant nursery stock in spring.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep pots, then transplant with minimal root disturbance. This selection bears warm brick-red spikes in early summer. Cut back after flowering for tidier growth and possible rebloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early or set out plants in spring.

directions: Scarify and start seed in deep cells to protect the developing taproot. This Band of Nobles selection produces clean creamy-white spikes. Deadhead promptly to extend the bloom season.

planting.note: Start indoors early or plant nursery stock in spring.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep pots for careful transplanting. This selection bears bright golden-yellow spikes above green palmate leaves. Remove spent stalks to encourage a second bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early; the dwarf Gallery series can bloom the first year.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells for early transplanting. This compact Gallery-series dwarf holds deep blue spikes on tidy plants ideal for containers and small borders. Deadhead to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: Start indoors early; dwarf series that may flower the first season.

directions: Scarify and start seed in deep pots before transplanting. This dwarf Gallery selection carries soft pink spikes on compact, well-branched plants. Cut spent spikes to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early; compact series suited to small spaces.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells for transplanting into well-drained soil. This dwarf Gallery selection bears rich red spikes on short, sturdy plants. Deadhead regularly to extend flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors early; dwarf habit good for containers.

directions: Scarify and start seed in deep pots to protect the taproot at transplant. This compact Gallery selection shows crisp white spikes on tidy plants. Remove faded flowers to keep the display going.

planting.note: Start indoors early; dwarf series flowers young.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells for early transplant. This dwarf Gallery selection produces soft yellow spikes on compact plants suited to the front border. Deadhead to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early or set out nursery plants in spring.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep pots for transplanting into rich, well-drained soil. This West Country selection blooms in a lively mix of pink, red, and yellow shades. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring; a vegetatively propagated West Country hybrid.

directions: Plant potted stock in spring in fertile, well-drained soil and keep the crown at grade. This West Country hybrid shows deep purple spikes flushed with orange. Remove faded spikes to prompt a second flush.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock in spring; propagate by cuttings to keep the color.

directions: Set potted plants in spring into rich, well-drained soil and water to establish. This West Country hybrid carries bicolor blue-and-white spikes on compact plants. Deadhead to extend the bloom.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock in spring; a tall West Country hybrid.

directions: Plant potted stock in spring in fertile, well-drained soil and stake tall stems if exposed. This West Country hybrid bears striking fiery red-orange spikes. Cut spent flower stalks to encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring; propagate by basal cuttings.

directions: Plant potted stock in spring in rich, well-drained soil and keep the crown level. This West Country hybrid produces deep red spikes with a lighter keel. Deadhead promptly for a second flush of bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors early or direct sow in spring; a tall Russell-type strain.

directions: Scarify seed and start in deep cells or sow directly in cool spring soil. This robust Russell-descended mix delivers tall spikes across the full color range. Deadhead spent stalks to keep plants vigorous.

planting.note: Fall sow or stratify seed; a low alpine and subalpine native.

directions: Scarify and cold-stratify seed, then sow a quarter inch deep in gritty, well-drained soil. This low, mat-forming native was among the first plants to recolonize Mount St. Helens. Compact blue-purple spikes bloom in early summer.

planting.note: Fall sow into deep sandy soil in the southeastern coastal plain.

directions: Scarify seed and sow shallowly in deep, sharply drained sand. This southeastern native has silvery foliage and resents transplanting, so sow it in place. Sky-blue spikes bloom in early spring.

planting.note: Raise from seed or divide established clumps in spring for the pure white form.

directions: Press seed lightly onto moist mix and keep it bright and warm to germinate. Set plants in fertile, well-drained soil and support the upright stems where wind is strong. The chalk-white flat clusters open in early summer and pair cleanly with darker perennials.

planting.note: Start indoors or divide clumps in early spring; the soft-pink form is a gentler alternative to the scarlet species.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of moist soil and keep it lightly warm until germination. Plant in a sunny bed with steady moisture and stake tall stems as needed. Expect the flat rose-pink heads to open through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Direct sow or set out transplants; the white form comes largely true and self-sows against its silver foliage.

directions: Sow onto gritty, free-draining soil and keep just moist until seedlings establish. The gray woolly foliage forms a winter rosette, followed by branching stems of pure white flowers in summer. Snip spent stems to limit reseeding or let a few ripen for next year.

planting.note: Direct sow the eyed form; white petals carry a pink center and it self-seeds readily.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on lean, well-drained soil and firm it gently. Silver-gray rosettes carry through winter and throw up airy stems of white flowers with a rosy-pink eye. Deadhead to prolong bloom, leaving a few pods if you want seedlings.

planting.note: Propagate by division or cuttings, as this sterile double does not set viable seed.

directions: Set divisions into gritty, sharply drained soil and water until roots take hold. Because the flowers are fully double and sterile they never go to seed, so bloom carries on longer with light deadheading. Enjoy the rich magenta double flowers over silver foliage from early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early for possible first-year bloom; bronze-purple foliage sets off the flowers.

directions: Sow indoors in late winter and grow on warm to encourage flowering the first season. Plant in full sun where the dark bronze leaves can contrast the blooms. Large scarlet-orange flowers open on short stems through summer; deadhead to extend the display.

planting.note: Start indoors for early bloom; this short-lived hybrid is often grown as a fresh sowing every couple of years.

directions: Sow onto moist mix and keep warm and bright until germination. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun for the strongest color. Oversized orange-scarlet flowers top compact stems in summer; replace or resow every few years as clumps tire.

planting.note: Also sold as Lychnis viscaria; start from seed or divide, forming tidy evergreen tufts.

directions: Surface-sow and keep lightly moist until seedlings appear. The grassy basal rosette carries sticky stems that trap crawling insects below the flowers. Magenta-pink blooms rise in early summer above neat evergreen foliage.

planting.note: Divide the sterile double form in spring, as it will not come true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in gritty, well-drained soil and keep watered until established. The fully double magenta flowers hold longer than the single species because they set little seed. Cut back spent flower stems to keep the evergreen tuft neat.

planting.note: Also known as Lychnis flos-cuculi; sow in damp ground for meadows and pond edges.

directions: Scatter seed on moist soil in sun or light shade and do not let it dry out. The finely cut, ragged-looking pink petals appear in late spring and early summer and draw butterflies. Best in consistently damp ground where it can naturalize.

planting.note: The white selection; raise from seed or division and keep the soil moist.

directions: Sow lightly on damp soil and keep moist until seedlings are up. Grow in a border or bog margin where it never bakes dry. Ragged, deeply fringed white flowers rise on airy stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A double-flowered form; propagate by division to keep it true.

directions: Divide crowns in spring and plant into reliably moist, humus-rich soil. The frilly, fully double rose-pink pompoms last well and rarely set seed. Trim spent stems and keep it watered through summer for the longest show.

planting.note: Start from seed or divide; forms silvery woolly mats topped by pink flower clusters.

directions: Sow onto well-drained mix and keep just moist until germination. The felted gray-white basal leaves make a good foil for the flowers. Rounded heads of soft rose-pink open in early to midsummer above the silvery mound.

planting.note: A compact deep-pink selection best increased by division.

directions: Set divisions into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. The low silver mat stays tidy and throws up short stems of intense rose-pink flowers. Shear after flowering to keep the foliage compact.

planting.note: A dwarf rock-garden species; seed benefits from a cool moist period before warmth.

directions: Sow in a gritty mix and give the pot several cold weeks before bringing it into warmth. Grow the small tuft in sharply drained soil in a trough or rock garden. Compact heads of pink flowers appear in late spring above the neat rosette.

planting.note: A gold-variegated form; increase by division so the leaf markings hold true.

directions: Divide clumps in spring and plant in moist, fertile soil with afternoon shade. The green leaves are irregularly edged and splashed with creamy gold, carrying pink flowers above. Remove any all-green shoots that revert to keep the variegation strong.

planting.note: A long-blooming double pink; propagate by division or cuttings.

directions: Set divisions into humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil in sun to part shade. The fully double rose-pink flowers repeat over a long season with regular deadheading. Cut plants back after the main flush to encourage fresh growth and rebloom.

planting.note: A short-lived meadow campion whose white flowers open toward evening and scent the air.

directions: Direct-sow on open, well-drained ground and press the seed into contact with the soil. Plants branch into leafy stems bearing white flowers that release fragrance at dusk to draw moths. Leave seed pods if you want it to persist, since individual plants are short-lived.

planting.note: A low coastal cushion for rock gardens and wall tops; tolerates salt and drought.

directions: Sow onto gritty mix and cover lightly, keeping just moist until germination. The trailing blue-green mat spills over walls and rocks and shrugs off salt spray. White flowers with inflated calyces open through early and midsummer above the cushion.

planting.note: Grown for cream-edged foliage; take cuttings or divide to keep the variegation.

directions: Root soft cuttings in gritty mix or divide the trailing mat in spring. The blue-green leaves carry a neat creamy-white margin that brightens rock gardens. White balloon-backed flowers appear in summer; remove plain green reversions as they show.

planting.note: A double white form; increase by division or cuttings.

directions: Divide the low cushion in spring and plant into sharply drained, gritty soil. The fully double white flowers sit on inflated calyces above trailing blue-green foliage. Ideal draped over a wall or the edge of a raised bed in full sun.

planting.note: A true cushion alpine; seed germinates best after a cold moist spell and plants are slow to flower.

directions: Give sown pots a cold, moist period, then grow on cool in very gritty, free-draining mix. The dense mossy cushion hugs the ground and takes a few years to settle before flowering well. Tiny stemless pink flowers stud the green mat in late spring; keep it cool and never waterlogged.

planting.note: A late-flowering rock-garden campion valued for bloom from summer into autumn.

directions: Sow lightly on gritty mix and keep just moist until seedlings appear. The low leafy tuft spreads slowly and blooms unusually late for a campion. Bright magenta-pink flowers cover the plant from mid or late summer through fall.

planting.note: A soft-pink selection; divide or take cuttings to keep the color true.

directions: Divide the tuft in spring and plant in sharply drained soil in full sun. The pale shell-pink flowers are softer than the species and continue from late summer into autumn. Trim lightly after the first flush to keep the mound compact.

planting.note: An easy hardy annual, also called None-so-pretty, that self-sows in the garden.

directions: Direct-sow in spring where plants are to grow and thin the seedlings once up. Flat clusters of bright pink flowers top blue-green stems in summer and draw butterflies. Leave a few heads to ripen and it will return from self-sown seed.

planting.note: A dainty hardy annual once listed as Viscaria or Lychnis coeli-rosa; good for cutting.

directions: Sow directly in cool spring soil and thin to avoid crowding. Slender stems carry saucer-shaped rose-pink flowers with a paler eye in early summer. Sow in succession for a longer run of blooms suited to small posies.

planting.note: A low, spreading hardy annual good for edging and containers.

directions: Direct-sow where plants are wanted and thin the seedlings to give them room. The bushy mounds carry nodding pale-pink flowers over a long summer season. Deadhead or shear lightly to keep fresh bloom coming and tidy the edge.

planting.note: A North American woodland-edge native with fringed white flowers; cold stratify the seed.

directions: Chill sown seed cold and moist, then grow on in humus-rich soil in light shade. Whorled leaves climb the upright stems below the flowers. Deeply fringed white flowers open in mid to late summer and suit a woodland garden.

planting.note: A western North American native, also called Mexican campion, with fringed scarlet flowers.

directions: Cold-stratify the seed, then grow seedlings in gritty, fast-draining soil. Plants prefer dry summers and resent wet, heavy ground. Deeply cut, ragged scarlet flowers open through summer and draw hummingbirds.

planting.note: A neat cushion for troughs and rock gardens; a cool moist spell helps the seed.

directions: Sow on gritty mix and give it a cool period before warmth to improve germination. The evergreen tuft stays low and tidy in sharply drained soil. Sprays of small white flowers with prettily toothed petals open in early summer.

planting.note: A double white form; increase by division or cuttings to keep it true.

directions: Divide the low cushion in spring and set into gritty, free-draining soil. The fully double white flowers last longer than the single form and rarely set seed. Ideal in a trough or raised alpine bed with sharp drainage.

planting.note: A leafy woodland campion from the Caucasus with inflated calyces and fringed white petals.

directions: Sow lightly and keep moist until seedlings appear, then grow on in humus-rich soil. Plants form a bold leafy clump that thrives in light shade at a border edge. White flowers with deeply fringed petals sit above balloon-like calyces in early summer.

planting.note: A compact Japanese species for rock gardens, flowering later in the season.

directions: Sow on gritty mix and keep just moist until germination. The low, tidy mound suits a rock garden or trough in sharply drained soil. Deep rose-pink flowers with notched petals open from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Divide clumps or set plants out in spring in moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown level with the soil surface and water in well. This species spreads by rhizomes, so site it where it can colonize or edge it with a barrier. Whorls of bright yellow star-shaped flowers ring the upright stems through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring; propagate only by division to keep the variegation.

directions: Plant rooted divisions in fertile, evenly moist soil and firm around the crown. New shoots emerge with pink-tinged edges that mature to cream-and-green variegation, so avoid harsh midday sun that can scorch the pale margins. Yellow flowers open along the leafy stems in summer above the patterned foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for the deepest leaf color.

directions: Site divisions in full sun so the foliage holds its dark chocolate-burgundy tone rather than fading green. Keep the soil evenly moist and divide every few years to manage its rhizomatous spread. Yellow flowers pop against the purple leaves in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Sow seed on the surface with a light dusting of soil and warmth, then grow on and set out after the last frost. Provide full sun and well-drained soil, as this short-lived perennial resents wet winter feet. Dense spikes of deep wine-purple flowers rise above silvery blue-green foliage all summer and attract bees.

planting.note: Plant clump-forming divisions in spring in moist but drained soil.

directions: Plant in reliably moist, well-drained soil where the upright clumps will not dry out. Unlike many loosestrifes it stays put in a tidy clump rather than running. Slender spires of grey-white flowers open from the base upward over grey-green willowy foliage in summer.

planting.note: Best in damp ground or pond margins; contain its running roots.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet soil at the edge of a pond or bog where it thrives. It spreads energetically by rhizomes and is considered invasive in some regions, so check local guidance before planting. Loose clusters of yellow flowers top the leafy stems through summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost; often grown as an annual spiller in containers.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in beds or trailing over the edge of a pot. Keep the soil evenly moist and pinch stems to encourage a fuller cascade. Bronze-purple foliage sets off clusters of yellow flowers from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost; commonly used as a container spiller and grown as an annual in cold zones.

directions: Set rooted cuttings out once nights stay mild and keep the soil consistently moist. The leaves are edged in gold and green and flush red-bronze in cool weather. Bright yellow flowers gather in the leaf axils over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant after frost as a groundcover or spiller; treat as an annual where winters are cold.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings after the last frost in moist, fertile soil. The low trailing stems root as they run and make a quick green carpet or container edge. Dense clusters of golden-yellow flowers crowd the stem tips from late spring onward.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist soil with room to run.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, sunny ground where its spreading roots can colonize. Water through dry weather to keep the foliage fresh. Curved, tapering spikes of small white flowers rise above the clump in summer, resembling a paler cousin of gooseneck loosestrife.

planting.note: Plant at pond edges or in saturated bog soil in spring.

directions: Set plants into wet muck along a pond margin or in shallow standing water where they naturalize. Keep the roots saturated at all times. Small tufted clusters of yellow flowers emerge from the leaf joints partway up the stem in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shaded ground as a low groundcover.

directions: Tuck rooted divisions into damp, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. The low creeping stems knit into a fine-textured mat over time. Small solitary yellow flowers sit in the leaf axils through late spring and summer.

planting.note: A North American native for moist meadows and stream banks; plant in spring.

directions: Plant in moist, average soil in sun or light shade where it can spread modestly by runners. Its narrow lance-shaped leaves distinguish it from broader-leaved relatives. Nodding yellow flowers with pointed petals appear along the stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring as a trailing groundcover in mild climates.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into moist, well-drained soil and let the slender stems trail and root as they go. This is the Chinese herb known as jin qian cao, valued in traditional medicine. Yellow flowers open in pairs from the leaf axils through late spring and summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter; a species preserved in cultivation after going extinct in the wild.

directions: Sow seed indoors and grow on before transplanting after frost into well-drained soil. This Mediterranean species survives only in gardens and seed banks, so saving your own seed helps keep it going. Small greenish-white to pale-pink flowers open along upright spikes above smooth foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist ground near water.

directions: Plant divisions in reliably moist soil at a pond edge or damp border. The upright reddish stems spread slowly by rhizomes into a colony. Slim erect spikes of small white flowers, often tinged pink, open in late summer.

planting.note: Sow on the surface or plant rhizome divisions near the last frost date.

directions: Scatter seed on firmed soil and leave it uncovered, watering gently to settle it in. This species runs even more freely at the root than common plume poppy, so plant it where its spread is welcome or contain the roots. Feathery bronze-pink plumes appear in summer above large lobed leaves; deadhead to reduce volunteer seedlings.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring; this sterile hybrid is not raised from seed.

directions: Set divisions with the crown just below the soil surface in well-drained ground and water until established. As a garden hybrid it must be propagated by division or root cuttings rather than seed. Tall stems carry soft buff to coral plumes in high summer above handsome grey-green foliage; stake in exposed sites to prevent wind flop.

planting.note: Increase this named selection only by division or root cuttings in spring.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep them watered through the first season. Because this is a named clone, propagate it vegetatively so the coral colouring stays true. Warm coral-pink plumes rise on statuesque stems from mid to late summer above deeply lobed foliage; deadhead and cut back hard in autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a named selection grown from division, not seed.

directions: Settle divisions into rich, well-drained soil and water regularly while roots take hold. Propagate by lifting and splitting the running rhizomes so the pink tone comes true. Airy buff-pink plumes flushed with rose open across summer above bold scalloped leaves; provide support in windy spots and shear spent flower stems.

planting.note: Divide in spring; the variegation holds only when propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in soil that stays reliably moist, and give a little shade so the marbled leaves do not scorch. Always increase this form by division since seedlings revert to plain green. The cream-splashed foliage is the main draw, topped by pale plumes in summer; remove any all-green shoots promptly to keep the variegation.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes or start early indoors.

directions: Press seed just under the surface and thin seedlings to stand about a foot and a half apart. Deadhead spent stalks to prolong the display and reduce self-seeding, which can be prolific. The pale flowers striped with deep maroon veins open across summer.

planting.note: Start indoors six weeks before the last frost for earliest bloom.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm trays and transplant once seedlings have several true leaves. This compact strain needs less staking than the species but still benefits from a shear after the first bloom flush. Lavender-blue veined flowers appear from early summer onward.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost since this low form is grown vegetatively.

directions: Plant this sprawling, low-growing selection where it can trail over a bank or wall edge. Take softwood cuttings in early summer to keep the true form, and trim lightly to shape after flowering. Pale blue veined blooms hug the ground through the warm months.

planting.note: Direct sow near the last frost date in well-drained ground.

directions: Sow seed where plants are to grow and thin to prevent crowding of the tall stems. This robust variety often reaches shoulder height and may need support in windy gardens. Rich rosy-purple flowers with darker veins bloom generously through summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in spring for bloom the first season.

directions: Cover seed lightly and grow seedlings on before setting out after frost. Pinch young plants once to build a bushier habit and deadhead to extend the season. Flowers open in a shifting mix of purple, blue, and mauve shades all summer.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring where the plants are to flower.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared soil and rake in lightly, thinning young plants to a hand's width apart. The finely cut foliage carries a faint musky scent when brushed, and cutting back after bloom often brings a second show. Soft pink saucer flowers appear through summer and self-sow gently.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; comes largely true from seed.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the bed evenly moist until seedlings establish. This pure white form blends easily into cottage and wildlife plantings and rarely needs staking. Clean white flowers with a satiny sheen bloom from early to late summer.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring for summer bloom.

directions: Barely cover the seed and thin seedlings once they crowd. Plants are short-lived but perpetuate themselves by self-sowing, so leave a few seed heads if you want them to persist. Warm rose-pink flowers cover the finely divided foliage through summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in average, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep and thin to give each plant space to bush out. The deep taproot resents disturbance, so start in place or move only while young. Bright pink notched-petal flowers open along upright stems from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Start indoors early or sow in place in spring.

directions: Grow seedlings on and set out in a sunny, freely draining spot. This narrow, upright form holds itself well and suits tighter borders without flopping. Clear pink flowers bloom over a long stretch from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Sow seed about half an inch deep in warm ground and thin to stand well apart. Grown as much for its edible mild leaves as its flowers, it grows quickly and tall in a single season. Small clustered pale flowers form in the leaf axils along the stems in summer.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant seed shallowly and thin seedlings to prevent crowding of the tall stems. The frilled, crimped leaf margins make it a decorative accent as well as a leaf crop. Insignificant whitish flowers cluster at the leaf joints through the warm season.

planting.note: Sow in spring; a cool spell aids even germination.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and be patient, as sprouting can be slow and uneven. This western native prefers full sun with steady moisture in spring and tolerates drier summers once settled. Spikes of pink cup-shaped flowers rise above the low foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out divisions or plants in spring for best establishment.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Deadhead the finished spikes to encourage a lighter second flush later in the season. Bright rose-pink hollyhock-like flowers crowd the upright stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this cultivar is grown vegetatively.

directions: Set plants into rich, well-drained soil and stake the tall spikes in exposed beds. Cut the whole clump back after flowering to tidy it and prompt fresh basal growth. Soft pink flowers with distinctively fringed petals bloom through mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant out in spring from divisions or container stock.

directions: Give this selection full sun and soil that stays lightly moist through summer. Remove spent flower stems promptly to lengthen the blooming period. Deep rose-pink flowers open along sturdy stems from early summer onward.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for a compact border plant.

directions: Plant this shorter cultivar toward the front of a sunny bed in fertile soil. Its compact habit rarely needs staking, and shearing after bloom keeps it neat. Rich purple-pink flowers cluster on tidy spikes through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, moisture-retentive spot.

directions: Set out plants where they get steady moisture and good light. Deadheading the pale spikes encourages further flowering into late summer. Delicate shell-pink flowers rise on compact stems above the divided foliage.

planting.note: Sow in spring; germination can be slow and staggered.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the seedbed cool and evenly damp. This meadow species spreads slowly by rhizomes and appreciates moist ground. Slender spikes of white flowers appear above glossy foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow in spring; a chilled sowing improves germination.

directions: Sow thinly and expect uneven sprouting over several weeks. This western native thrives in damp meadows and streamside ground with full sun. Pink flowers on tall wands attract bees from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile, well-drained soil and divide every third year to renew vigor. Cut back the spent spikes to keep the clump tidy and prompt rebloom. Vivid carmine-rose flowers stand on upright stems through summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring for a tall border accent.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with reliable summer moisture and stake the tall spikes if needed. Removing finished flower stems extends the season and prevents self-sowing. Deep rose-pink flowers rise on strong stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and moist soil.

directions: Set plants into rich soil that does not dry out through the growing season. Cut the whole plant back after the main flush to encourage fresh basal foliage. Warm salmon-pink flowers crowd the spikes through summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in consistently moist ground.

directions: Sow seed shallowly where the soil stays reliably damp, as this coastal species dislikes drought. Give it full sun and rich soil for the strongest spikes. Rich pink flowers cluster densely on tall stems in summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring in a sunny, seasonally moist site.

directions: Sow thinly and keep the seedbed moist while plants establish their deep roots. This western native handles alkaline soil and tolerates dry spells once mature. Pink to rose flowers open along slender spikes in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in early spring and set plants out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it into moist soil, since light aids germination. Give plants steady moisture and good airflow to limit powdery mildew, and cut spent stems to encourage rebloom. Ragged crowns of tubular scarlet flowers open through mid to late summer and draw hummingbirds.

planting.note: Direct-sow after frost or start indoors; cold-moist stratification improves sprouting.

directions: Chill the seed in moist medium for a few weeks before sowing to break dormancy, then barely cover it. This prairie native tolerates dry, lean soil once established and rarely needs feeding. Lavender pom-pom blooms appear in midsummer and are a magnet for native bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Direct-sow after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Scatter seed on warm, well-drained soil and press in gently without covering. This fast annual blooms the first season and reseeds to return year after year. Stacked tiers of lavender-pink flowers carry a lemony mint scent from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall or spring; cold conditioning helps germination.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist period before sowing and barely cover it. This compact, early-blooming native shrugs off drought and shows better mildew resistance than most bee balms. Pale pink flowers speckled with purple open in late spring, weeks ahead of other Monarda.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, airy site.

directions: Space plants generously to keep foliage dry and mildew-free, a trait this Canadian introduction is known for. Water during dry spells and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Clear rose-pink flowerheads open across midsummer and feed bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in spring.

directions: Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and mulch to keep the roots cool. This classic old cultivar spreads by runners, so lift and divide it regularly to control the clump. Brilliant scarlet flowers open in midsummer and are a longtime favorite of hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil and water consistently to reduce mildew stress. This selection combines large red blooms with good disease resistance. Big scarlet flowerheads carry from mid to late summer and draw hummingbirds and bees.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Keep this old English cultivar in rich, evenly moist soil and provide good airflow. Deadhead faded heads to prolong the display and cut back hard after flowering. Soft rose-pink whorls open in midsummer above aromatic foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; also sold as 'Blue Stocking'.

directions: Give this vigorous German selection moist, fertile soil and room to spread. Water in dry weather and divide every two or three years to keep it blooming freely. Rich violet-purple flowerheads open in midsummer and attract clouds of pollinators.

planting.note: Plant this compact form in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: This dwarf, mildew-resistant selection stays tidy and needs no staking. Plant in average soil, water during drought, and shear lightly after the first flush. Lavender-pink flowers cover the low mound in midsummer, ideal for the front of a border.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist soil in full sun.

directions: Set plants in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with good air movement around the leaves. This tall, sturdy cultivar resists mildew well and rarely needs staking. Deep wine-red flowers open on strong stems from mid to late summer and lure hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, open spot.

directions: Grow in moist, fertile soil with plenty of airflow to keep foliage clean. This mildew-tolerant selection blooms freely and benefits from division every few years. Clear violet flowerheads open across midsummer above fragrant foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; also sold as 'Snow White' or 'Snow Maiden'.

directions: Site this white-flowered German cultivar in moist soil with good ventilation. Water in dry spells and remove spent heads to keep it neat. Crisp white flowers with green-tinged bracts open in midsummer and glow in the evening garden.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; also sold as 'Prairie Night'.

directions: Grow this tall German selection in moist, fertile soil with room for air to move. Divide clumps periodically and water during drought to sustain flowering. Deep violet-purple flowerheads rise on tall stems from midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Set plants in rich, moist soil and keep foliage airy to fend off mildew. Deadhead to extend bloom and divide every few years to renew vigor. Bright coral-pink flowers stand out in midsummer and attract hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, open border.

directions: This mildew-resistant cultivar wants moist, fertile soil and good airflow. Water in dry weather and divide clumps to keep them dense and floriferous. Deep purple flowers hold their color well through mid and late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in moist soil.

directions: Grow this tall selection in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with open spacing. Its strong mildew resistance keeps foliage clean into late season. Deep red-purple flowerheads open on upright stems in midsummer and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: This refined English cultivar prefers rich, moist soil and good air movement. Deadhead spent flowers and divide the clump every few years. Soft pink flowers emerge from dusky purple bracts in midsummer for a two-tone effect.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with even moisture.

directions: Set plants in moist, fertile soil and keep them airy to limit mildew. Water in drought and divide clumps to sustain flowering. Dark, deep red flowers with a mahogany cast open in midsummer above aromatic leaves.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf form in spring at the front of a border.

directions: This tidy dwarf needs no staking and shows good mildew resistance. Plant in average, moist soil and water during dry stretches. Deep purple flowers cover the compact mound in midsummer and draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny front-of-border position.

directions: Grow this compact selection in average, moist soil with decent airflow. It stays short and neat without pinching or staking. Rosy-pink flowers blanket the mound from midsummer, perfect for small gardens and containers.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf form in spring in full sun.

directions: This low, mildew-resistant cultivar is well suited to the front of a bed or a pot. Plant in moist, average soil and water when the weather turns dry. Vivid cerise-red flowers cover the compact plant in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant this Balmy-series dwarf in spring in full sun.

directions: This very compact selection flowers early and needs no staking. Plant in average, moist soil and water during dry weather. Purple flowers cover the low mound from early summer, well suited to containers and small beds.

planting.note: Plant in spring at the front of a sunny border.

directions: A dwarf, early-blooming form that stays compact without pinching. Plant in moist, average soil and give occasional deep watering. Soft pink flowers top the low mound from early summer.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf in spring in full sun.

directions: This low Balmy-series selection blooms early and stays neat in small spaces. Plant in average, moist soil and water in dry spells. Rich rose flowers cover the compact plant from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny front-of-border spot.

directions: A compact, early dwarf that works well in containers and small beds. Plant in moist, average soil and water when rainfall is short. Lilac-purple flowers blanket the mound from early summer.

planting.note: Plant this Sugar Buzz selection in spring in full sun.

directions: This mid-sized, mildew-resistant selection holds strong stems without staking. Plant in moist, fertile soil and water during dry weather. Bright cherry-red flowers cover the plant in midsummer and attract hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in moist soil.

directions: A compact, disease-resistant selection with big flowers on tidy plants. Grow in moist, fertile soil and water when dry. Large fuchsia-purple flowerheads open in midsummer and pull in pollinators.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf in spring at the front of a sunny border.

directions: This low, floriferous selection carries oversized blooms on a short frame. Plant in moist, average soil and water during drought. Rich lavender-purple flowers cover the compact plant in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant this Sugar Buzz selection in spring in full sun.

directions: A mildew-resistant, mid-height selection that needs no staking. Plant in moist, fertile soil and water during dry periods. Bright bubblegum-pink flowers cover the plant in midsummer and feed bees and hummingbirds.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf red in spring in full sun.

directions: This compact selection resists mildew and stays upright without support. Plant in moist, average soil and water during dry weather. Deep red flowers cover the low plant in midsummer, striking near the front of a bed.

planting.note: Plant this very short form in spring at the front edge.

directions: One of the shortest bee balms, ideal for edging and containers. Plant in moist, average soil and water when rainfall is scarce. Pale pink flowers cover the miniature mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf in spring in a sunny border.

directions: A low, spreading selection that blooms heavily on compact plants. Grow in moist, average soil and water during dry stretches. Plum-purple flowers cover the mound from midsummer, well suited to the front of a bed.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf in spring in full sun.

directions: This compact, free-flowering selection needs no staking. Plant in moist, average soil and water in dry weather. Soft lilac flowers blanket the low plant from midsummer.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf in spring at the border front.

directions: A low, mounding selection that flowers heavily on tidy plants. Plant in moist, average soil and water during drought. Rosy flowers cover the compact mound from midsummer and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun in moist soil.

directions: This tall selection wants moist, fertile soil with open spacing for airflow. Water in dry spells and divide clumps to keep them vigorous. Pale pinkish-white flowers open on tall stems in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, open border.

directions: Grow this selection in moist, fertile soil with good ventilation. Deadhead to extend bloom and divide the clump every few years. Lavender-purple flowers with showy bracts open in midsummer above aromatic foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in moist soil.

directions: This tall, robust selection prefers moist, fertile soil and room for air to move. Water during drought and divide clumps to renew vigor. Violet-purple flowers with dark bracts rise on tall stems in midsummer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors in fall or spring in light woodland shade.

directions: Give seed a cold-moist period and sow it shallowly in humus-rich soil. This woodland native tolerates part shade and moist conditions better than most bee balms. Whitish flowers tinged with pink open in early to midsummer for shady pollinator plantings.

planting.note: This sterile hybrid is grown from divisions or potted plants, not seed.

directions: Set divisions in spring in full sun with lean, well-drained soil, spacing them for good air flow. Because the hybrid is sterile it blooms long and heavily; shear the whole plant back after the first wave to keep it tidy and reblooming. Silvery gray-green foliage carries clouds of soft blue flowers from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Propagated by division or cuttings; set out potted plants in spring.

directions: Plant this large, billowing catmint in full sun with room to sprawl, keeping the soil lean and well drained. It makes a bold edging or filler; shear it back by two-thirds after flowering to renew the mound and spark a second flush. Tall arching stems carry deep violet-blue flowers over gray-green leaves through early and midsummer.

planting.note: A sterile compact form grown from cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set this dwarf, seedless version of Walker's Low in full sun with average, well-drained soil. It stays tidy and rarely needs shearing, though a light trim after the first bloom keeps it fresh. Compact mounds carry lavender-blue flowers non-stop from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Grown from divisions or cuttings; plant in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this compact catmint in a sunny, well-drained bed where its neat habit works well along paths. Cut it back after the first bloom to keep the clump dense and encourage rebloom. Short spikes of blue-violet flowers rise above aromatic foliage from early summer onward.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring; hybrid rarely seeds true.

directions: This cold-hardy Canadian selection wants full sun and gritty, well-drained soil. Shear it back after flowering to renew the gray-green mound and prompt a second bloom. Upright spikes of lavender-blue flowers appear from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions or cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this compact, free-flowering catmint in full sun with lean, well-drained soil. Its low, spreading habit suits front borders and edges; shear after the first bloom to keep it neat and reblooming. Deep blue flower spikes cover the silvery foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Vegetatively propagated selection; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Set this tidy, dome-shaped catmint in full sun with average, well-drained soil. It holds a uniform mound without flopping and needs little more than a shear once flowering slows to encourage repeat bloom. Rich blue flowers smother the aromatic foliage from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; set out potted plants in spring.

directions: Plant this dwarf catmint in full sun where its dense, rounded habit fits small spaces and containers. Flowers form all the way down to the base, so it looks full without shearing, though a trim refreshes it. Blue blooms with a rosy calyx cover the compact mound from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Vegetatively propagated; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Give this compact selection full sun and well-drained soil for the best flower color. Its neat mounding shape resists flopping; shear lightly after the main flush to spur reblooming. Periwinkle-blue flowers with dark rose calyces open from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this foliage-focused catmint in full sun to bring out its bright chartreuse leaf color. Well-drained soil keeps it vigorous; trim spent flowers to keep the mound looking clean. Lavender-blue blooms contrast with the golden-green foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; set out potted plants in spring.

directions: Use this true miniature catmint for edging, troughs, and small gravel gardens in full sun. Sharp drainage keeps the tiny mound compact; a light shear after bloom tidies it. Short lavender-blue spikes appear over fine gray-green foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this low, spreading catmint in full sun with lean, well-drained soil as a groundcover or edger. It weaves nicely between other perennials; shear after flowering for a repeat show. Purple-blue flowers blanket the trailing gray-green foliage in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for bloom the first season.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it cool and lightly moist until seedlings appear over a few weeks. Grow in full sun with good drainage; the strongly veined leaves set off the flowers well. Dense spikes of soft blue flowers rise on a compact clump through summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year flowers.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a well-drained mix and keep barely moist until germination. Plant in full sun and deadhead to keep the compact clump blooming. Short spikes of clear rosy-pink flowers top the veined foliage through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter or sow outdoors once soil warms.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface and keep evenly moist until sprouting. This tidy, non-floppy species prefers full sun and sharp drainage and stays more clumped than spreading. Dense spikes of blue flowers rise above conspicuously veined green leaves in summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or set out divisions in spring.

directions: Start seed indoors on the soil surface and grow on before planting out in full sun to light shade. This larger species tolerates a bit more moisture than most catmints but still wants good drainage. Airy branching spikes of violet-blue flowers with dark calyces bloom from early summer, reblooming if cut back.

planting.note: Grown from divisions; plant potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant this taller catmint in full sun to light shade in average, well-drained soil, allowing room for the upright clump. Cut it back after the first bloom to keep it upright and prompt fresh flowers. Soft dusky-pink flowers held in mauve-purple calyces give an unusual two-tone effect through summer.
